questions.question: Explain how the Z-buffer algorithm handles the visibility problem in 3D rendering and discuss its advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: Z-buffer stores depth values for each pixel in a separate buffer
For each fragment, compare its depth with stored depth - draw if closer, discard if farther
Advantages: handles intersecting polygons, parallelizable, handles dynamic scenes
Limitations: requires additional memory, aliasing artifacts at far distances, no transparency handling without additional passes

questions.question: Compare and contrast immediate mode and retained mode rendering. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Immediate mode: sends commands each frame, no scene graph storage, more control, higher CPU overhead
Retained mode: maintains persistent scene graph, automatic updates, easier development, less flexibility
Choose immediate mode for: games needing maximum performance, dynamic geometry, custom rendering
Choose retained mode for: GUI applications, static scenes, rapid prototyping

questions.question: Describe the rendering pipeline architecture and explain the purpose of each major stage from vertex processing to framebuffer operations.
keyPoints: Vertex Processing: transformation, lighting, and projection of vertices
Primitive Assembly: assembles vertices into points, lines, or triangles
Rasterization: converts primitives into fragments (potential pixels)
Fragment Processing: applies texturing, shading, and per-fragment operations
Framebuffer Operations: depth testing, stencil testing, blending, and writing to framebuffer

questions.question: How does the Painter's Algorithm work for hidden surface removal, and what are its major limitations that necessitate more advanced techniques like Z-buffer or BSP trees?
keyPoints: Painter's Algorithm sorts all polygons by depth (typically farthest vertex or centroid)
Polygons are rendered from back to front, closer polygons overwrite farther ones
Limitation 1: fails with intersecting polygons where no clear depth order exists
Limitation 2: requires sorting all polygons - O(n log n) complexity for n polygons
Limitation 3: cyclic overlap patterns cannot be resolved by simple sorting
Advanced methods like Z-buffer handle these cases more robustly at per-pixel level

questions.question: Explain how BSP (Binary Space Partitioning) trees solve the visibility problem in rendering and discuss their advantages in static vs dynamic scenes.
keyPoints: BSP recursively subdivides space using partitioning planes
Each node splits geometry into front and back subsets relative to the plane
For visibility: traverse tree in order based on viewer's position to render back-to-front
Static scenes: tree built once, very fast visibility queries
Dynamic scenes: tree must be rebuilt or updated when geometry changes - expensive
Advantage: provides efficient visibility determination without per-pixel depth comparisons
